planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in moist, sandy, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; myrtle holly is dioecious — plant male and female plants for berry production. Seeds require double dormancy stratification and are most practically grown from cuttings.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, sandy, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun in zones 7–10; myrtle holly is a native evergreen shrub of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain, named for its unusually small, narrow, glossy leaves that strongly resemble those of true myrtle — quite different from the larger leaves of most native hollies. It grows naturally in wet pine flatwoods, pond margins, bogs, and bay swamp edges throughout Florida and the Gulf Coast states, making it one of the most ecologically appropriate native shrubs for zone 9a moist-site plantings. The small, bright red to orange-red berries are highly attractive to bluebirds, yellow-rumped warblers, hermit thrushes, and other fruit-eating birds throughout winter. An underused native alternative to Japanese holly (I. crenata) that offers far greater wildlife value and superior adaptation to Gulf Coast conditions.

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root plants in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; witherod viburnum spreads by suckers to form dense thickets in moist conditions. Fruit production is better with multiple plants. Cuttings root readily in summer.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; witherod viburnum is a native shrub of moist, acidic habitats across eastern North America — bog edges, streambanks, moist meadows, and pond margins from Newfoundland to Alabama. The berries pass through an extraordinary sequential progression of colors as they ripen — starting green, then turning through pink, blue-pink, and finally blue-black — so that clusters simultaneously display three or four colors. The fully ripe blue-black berries, called 'wild raisin,' are edible and sweet enough to eat raw or dried. Spectacular scarlet to burgundy fall foliage adds further season-long interest. Plant 2 or more plants of different clones for improved berry set. An exceptional wildlife shrub offering berries for over 30 bird species and the flowers feed numerous native bees and pollinators.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in moist to wet, acidic, sandy soil in partial shade; like all winterberries, this species is dioecious — plant at least one male for every 5–6 female plants for good fruit set. Smooth winterberry is slightly more compact than common winterberry (I. verticillata).

directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in partial shade to full sun; smooth winterberry is a native deciduous holly of the Atlantic Coastal Plain, closely related to but slightly smaller and more southern-adapted than common winterberry (I. verticillata). The glossy leaves and more tapered, smooth-margined leaflets distinguish it from the more northern species. In fall, the bright orange-red to scarlet berries are produced in exceptional abundance — smaller than I. verticillata berries but often in denser clusters — and persist into winter. The plant grows naturally in bog edges, wet flatwoods, and pond margins from Maine south to Georgia, making it well-suited to zone 9a moist conditions where it is more reliable than its northern relative. The berries are critically important winter food for bluebirds, robins, and cedar waxwings. Like all hollies, the berries are toxic to humans.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; canton fairy bells spreads gently by rhizomes to form expanding colonies in shaded garden conditions. More heat-tolerant than most Asian Disporum species.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in zones 7–10; canton fairy bells is an arching, bamboo-like woodland perennial from southern China and Southeast Asia, producing elegant, leafy stems that arch gracefully under the weight of pendant, tubular white-to-pale-pink flowers in late spring. The stems have an unusual multi-branching habit near the tips, creating a feathery, tiered silhouette in the shade garden. Black berries follow the flowers in fall and are consumed by thrushes and other berry-eating birds. The cultivar 'Night Heron' features striking dark purple-black stems that provide exceptional winter interest after the leaves drop. This species is notably more heat-tolerant than the Japanese species D. sessile, making it a superior choice for zone 7–10 shade gardens. Thrives in the same conditions as hostas, ferns, and hellebores.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination after natural cold stratification; or cold stratify seeds for 60 days and sow in late winter. Waterleaf grows naturally in rich, moist woodland soils in deep shade and self-sows readily in appropriate conditions.

directions: Direct sow in fall or plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in deep shade to partial shade; blunt-leaved waterleaf is a native woodland ephemeral-like perennial of rich forest floors across eastern North America, valued as an early spring edible — the young, furled leaves before flowering are among the most easily harvested native spring greens, with a mild flavor suitable for salads, soups, and sautéing as a pot herb. Unlike Virginia waterleaf (H. virginianum) which has sharply pointed leaflets, this species has distinctly rounded, blunt leaflet tips. The white to pale lavender flowers in spring attract specialist native bees. The plant dies back in summer heat, so it pairs naturally with summer-active ferns and hostas that fill in as it goes dormant. An excellent deep-shade groundcover for moist, woodland gardens that doubles as a spring salad green.
